examples.title: Algorithm: Producer-Consumer Problem
examples.description: Pseudocode solution using semaphores for the bounded buffer problem where producers add items and consumers remove them
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Producer-Consumer
INPUT: Buffer of size N, Producer processes, Consumer processes
OUTPUT: Synchronized access to shared buffer

// Global Variables:
// mutex: Binary semaphore = 1 (mutual exclusion)
// empty: Counting semaphore = N (empty slots)
// full: Counting semaphore = 0 (filled slots)
// buffer: Array of size N

PROCEDURE Producer()
BEGIN
 WHILE true DO
 item = produce_item() // Produce new data item
 
 wait(empty) // Wait if buffer is full
 wait(mutex) // Enter critical section
 
 add_item_to_buffer(item) // Add item to buffer
 
 signal(mutex) // Exit critical section
 signal(full) // Increment full slots count
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE Consumer()
BEGIN
 WHILE true DO
 wait(full) // Wait if buffer is empty
 wait(mutex) // Enter critical section
 
 item = remove_item_from_buffer() // Remove item from buffer
 
 signal(mutex) // Exit critical section
 signal(empty) // Increment empty slots count
 
 consume_item(item) // Process the item
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This solution uses three semaphores: mutex for mutual exclusion, empty to track available slots, and full to track filled slots. The producer waits for an empty slot, adds an item, and signals a full slot. The consumer waits for a full slot, removes an item, and signals an empty slot.

examples.title: Python: Producer-Consumer Implementation
examples.description: Thread-based implementation using Python's threading and semaphore primitives
examples.code:
import threading
import time
import random
from collections import deque

# Shared buffer and semaphores
buffer = deque(maxlen=5) # Buffer size = 5
mutex = threading.Semaphore(1) # Binary semaphore for mutual exclusion
empty = threading.Semaphore(5) # Counting semaphore for empty slots
full = threading.Semaphore(0) # Counting semaphore for full slots

# Item counter for tracking
item_count = 0

def producer(producer_id):
 global item_count
 while True:
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5)) # Simulate production time
 
 item = item_count # Produce item
 item_count += 1
 
 empty.acquire() # Wait for empty slot
 mutex.acquire() # Enter critical section
 
 buffer.append(item)
 print(f"Producer {producer_id} produced: {item}. Buffer: {list(buffer)}")
 
 mutex.release() # Exit critical section
 full.release() # Signal full slot

def consumer(consumer_id):
 while True:
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5)) # Simulate consumption time
 
 full.acquire() # Wait for full slot
 mutex.acquire() # Enter critical section
 
 item = buffer.popleft()
 print(f"Consumer {consumer_id} consumed: {item}. Buffer: {list(buffer)}")
 
 mutex.release() # Exit critical section
 empty.release() # Signal empty slot

# Create producer and consumer threads
producers = [threading.Thread(target=producer, args=(i,)) for i in range(2)]
consumers = [threading.Thread(target=consumer, args=(i,)) for i in range(2)]

# Start all threads
for p in producers:
 p.start()
for c in consumers:
 c.start()

# Keep main thread alive (in real use, add proper termination)
for t in producers + consumers:
 t.join()
examples.explanation: Python's threading module provides Semaphore class. The solution demonstrates the producer-consumer pattern with bounded buffer. The mutex ensures only one thread modifies the buffer at a time, while empty and full semaphores coordinate between producers and consumers. Time complexity for each operation is O(1).
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) where N is buffer size

examples.title: C: Producer-Consumer Implementation
examples.description: POSIX threads implementation using semaphores and mutex for synchronization
examples.explanation: This C implementation uses POSIX threads (pthread.h) and semaphores (sem.h). The circular buffer approach with in/out indices prevents overflow/underflow. The sem_wait() and sem_post() operations are atomic, ensuring proper synchronization. Compile with: gcc -pthread producer_consumer.c -o producer_consumer
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) where N is buffer size

examples.title: Algorithm: Readers-Writers Problem
examples.description: Pseudocode for the first readers-writers problem with readers having priority
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Readers-Writers (First Variation - Readers Priority)
INPUT: Shared data area, Reader processes, Writer processes
OUTPUT: Synchronized read/write access with multiple concurrent readers

// Global Variables:
// mutex: Binary semaphore = 1 (protects read_count)
// wrt: Binary semaphore = 1 (for writer mutual exclusion)
// read_count: Integer = 0 (number of active readers)

PROCEDURE Reader()
BEGIN
 WHILE true DO
 wait(mutex); // Enter critical section for read_count
 read_count = read_count + 1
 
 IF read_count == 1 THEN
 wait(wrt); // First reader blocks writers
 END IF
 
 signal(mutex); // Exit critical section
 
 // Perform reading operation (multiple readers allowed)
 read_data();
 
 wait(mutex); // Enter critical section for read_count
 read_count = read_count - 1
 
 IF read_count == 0 THEN
 signal(wrt); // Last reader releases writers
 END IF
 
 signal(mutex); // Exit critical section
 
 // Process data
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE Writer()
BEGIN
 WHILE true DO
 wait(wrt); // Request exclusive access
 
 // Perform writing operation (exclusive access)
 write_data();
 
 signal(wrt); // Release exclusive access
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This solution gives readers priority - once a reader starts, subsequent readers can join. The first reader locks the wrt semaphore to prevent writers, and the last reader releases it. This can cause writer starvation but ensures maximum read throughput. Writers must wait until all readers finish.

examples.title: Python: Dining Philosophers Solution
examples.description: Implementation with deadlock avoidance using odd/even strategy and limited philosophers
examples.code:
import threading
import time
import random

NUM_PHILOSOPHERS = 5

# Semaphores for chopsticks (initially 1 each)
chopsticks = [threading.Semaphore(1) for _ in range(NUM_PHILOSOPHERS)]

# Solution 1: Allow only 4 philosophers at the table
# This prevents deadlock by ensuring at least one chopstick is available
limit = threading.Semaphore(4)

# Solution 2: Odd/even asymmetric solution
# Odd philosophers pick left then right, even pick right then left

def philosopher_naive(philosopher_id):
 """Naive solution - can lead to deadlock"""
 left = philosopher_id
 right = (philosopher_id + 1) % NUM_PHILOSOPHERS
 
 while True:
 print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is thinking")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))
 
 # Pick up chopsticks
 chopsticks[left].acquire()
 chopsticks[right].acquire()
 
 print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is eating")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.0))
 
 # Put down chopsticks
 chopsticks[left].release()
 chopsticks[right].release()

def philosopher_with_limit(philosopher_id):
 """Solution with limited philosophers - prevents deadlock"""
 left = philosopher_id
 right = (philosopher_id + 1) % NUM_PHILOSOPHERS
 
 while True:
 print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is thinking")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))
 
 limit.acquire() # Limit to 4 philosophers
 chopsticks[left].acquire()
 chopsticks[right].acquire()
 
 print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is eating")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.0))
 
 chopsticks[left].release()
 chopsticks[right].release()
 limit.release()

def philosopher_asymmetric(philosopher_id):
 """Asymmetric solution - odd picks left first, even picks right first"""
 left = philosopher_id
 right = (philosopher_id + 1) % NUM_PHILOSOPHERS
 
 while True:
 print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is thinking")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))
 
 if philosopher_id % 2 == 1: # Odd philosopher
 chopsticks[left].acquire()
 chopsticks[right].acquire()
 else: # Even philosopher
 chopsticks[right].acquire()
 chopsticks[left].acquire()
 
 print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is eating")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.0))
 
 chopsticks[left].release()
 chopsticks[right].release()

# Run the asymmetric solution
threads = []
for i in range(NUM_PHILOSOPHERS):
 t = threading.Thread(target=philosopher_asymmetric, args=(i,))
 threads.append(t)
 t.start()

for t in threads:
 t.join()
examples.explanation: Three solutions are provided: (1) Naive - can deadlock if all pick left simultaneously, (2) Limited philosophers - allows only 4 to prevent circular wait, (3) Asymmetric - breaks symmetry by having odd/even philosophers pick chopsticks in different order. The asymmetric solution is efficient and deadlock-free.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) where N is number of philosophers

examples.title: C: Dining Philosophers with Monitor Solution
examples.description: C implementation using mutex and semaphores with resource hierarchy solution
examples.explanation: This is the Dijkstra solution using a state-based approach with a monitor (mutex). Each philosopher tests if they can eat (both neighbors not eating). If not, they wait on their own semaphore. When a philosopher finishes, they check neighbors who might now be able to eat. This solution prevents both deadlock and starvation. Compile with: gcc -pthread dining_philosophers.c -o dining_philosophers
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) for state and semaphore arrays

examples.title: Python: Sleeping Barber Problem
examples.description: Implementation with semaphores simulating barber shop with waiting chairs
examples.code:
import threading
import time
import random

# Configuration
NUM_CHAIRS = 3 # Number of waiting chairs
NUM_CUSTOMERS = 10 # Total customers to serve

# Semaphores
customers = threading.Semaphore(0) # Customers waiting
barber = threading.Semaphore(0) # Barber ready
mutex = threading.Lock() # Protect waiting count

# Shared variables
waiting = 0 # Number of customers waiting
customers_served = 0
customers_left = 0

def barber_shop():
 """Barber process - cuts hair or sleeps"""
 global waiting, customers_served
 
 while customers_served < NUM_CUSTOMERS:
 print("Barber is sleeping...")
 customers.acquire() # Sleep if no customers
 
 # Wake up and get customer
 with mutex:
 waiting -= 1
 print(f"Barber woke up. Waiting: {waiting}")
 barber.release() # Signal barber is ready
 
 # Cut hair
 print("Barber is cutting hair...")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 1.5))
 customers_served += 1
 print(f"Haircut complete! Served: {customers_served}")

def customer(customer_id):
 """Customer process - get haircut or leave"""
 global waiting, customers_left
 
 with mutex:
 if waiting < NUM_CHAIRS:
 waiting += 1
 print(f"Customer {customer_id} sat down. Waiting: {waiting}")
 customers.release() # Wake barber if sleeping
 mutex.release()
 
 barber.acquire() # Wait for barber
 print(f"Customer {customer_id} getting haircut")
 else:
 print(f"Customer {customer_id} left - no chairs available")
 customers_left += 1
 mutex.release()

# Run simulation
print("Barber shop opened!")

# Start barber thread
barber_thread = threading.Thread(target=barber_shop)
barber_thread.start()

# Create customer threads with random arrival times
customer_threads = []
for i in range(NUM_CUSTOMERS):
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.5)) # Random arrival
 t = threading.Thread(target=customer, args=(i,))
 customer_threads.append(t)
 t.start()

# Wait for all customers
for t in customer_threads:
 t.join()

# Wait for barber to finish
barber_thread.join()

print(f"\nShop closed! Customers served: {customers_served}, Customers left: {customers_left}")
examples.explanation: This implementation uses three semaphores: customers (counts waiting customers), barber (signals barber availability), and mutex (protects waiting counter). When no customers arrive, the barber sleeps on customers.acquire(). When a customer arrives, they either wait or leave if chairs are full. The barber semaphore ensures synchronization between customer and barber.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) where N is number of chairs
